doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 3 mm on image 27 (unchanged).
Left upper lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 4 mm on image 36 (unchanged).
In both lungs, solid micronodules measuring up to 2 mm (unchanged). Small bilateral apical "caps" are visualized (unchanged). Local pleural thickening in the right middle lobe, series 5, images 115-145 (unchanged). Calcified granulomas measuring up to 3 mm in both lungs (unchanged). Walls of some bronchi are unevenly thickened, with bronchial contents in the lumens of some bronchi. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Calcifications are present in a few nonenlarged intrathoracic lymph nodes.

Heart: Cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic index 0.52). Mild pericardial effusion measuring up to 8 mm. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies at the T1-T12 level up to +121 HU (signs of demineralization).

Upper abdomen: In the liver, calcifications measuring up to 2 mm.
doc_impression:
1. Micronodules in both lungs and solid nodules in both upper lobes, stable compared with CT dated September 03, 2025; per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, additional CT follow-up is not recommended. Bronchial contents and bronchial wall thickening in both lungs, which may be consistent with bronchitis. Calcified granulomas in both lungs and calcification of intrathoracic lymph nodes, consistent with prior infection (unchanged compared with CT dated September 03, 2025). Small bilateral apical "caps" (unchanged compared with CT dated September 03, 2025).

2. Cardiomegaly. Mild pericardial effusion. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Liver calcifications. No further evaluation is required.

val_protocol:
INDICATION: Lung nodules, shortness of breath.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on September 3, 2025, May 14, 2024. 

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 3.21 mGy; total DLP 120 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: There is interval increase in pleural effusions, measuring up to 2.9 cm on the right (previously 1.1 cm) and up to 2.1 cm on the left (previously not identified).
Against this background, the adjacent lung is compressed with subsegmental atelectatic changes in segments 9 and 10 on the right and segments 8, 9, and 10 on the left (increased extent compared with CT dated December 02, 2024).
Lung aeration is heterogeneous due to ground-glass opacity with vascular prominence and interlobular septal thickening, more pronounced in the upper lungs (without significant change).
Bilateral areas of centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema.
No nodules requiring follow-up are identified in the setting of respiratory motion artifact.
No infiltrative changes.
The trachea and major bronchi are patent. Bronchial walls are diffusely calcified.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: The heart is enlarged due to enlargement of all chambers.
Pericardial effusion is present, with separation of the pericardial layers measuring up to 1.1 cm at the level of the right atrium (previously 6 mm).
Stents are present in the left anterior descending, circumflex, and right coronary arteries. Against this background, coronary calcification cannot be excluded.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Aortic walls are diffusely calcified.
The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.2 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: No destructive osseous changes.
Moderate degenerative changes of the spine within the scanned field, spondylosis.
Decreased density of vertebral bodies within the scanned field to +62 HU.

Upper abdomen: Diffuse thickening of the left adrenal gland, measuring up to 1.2 cm in total thickness.
doc_impression:
1. Moderate mixed emphysema. No significant change compared with CT dated December 03, 2024.

2. Congestive interstitial changes in the lungs, bilateral pleural effusions, minimal pericardial effusion, and cardiomegaly. Worsening compared with CT dated December 03, 2024.

3. Compressive atelectatic changes in the lower lobes of both lungs due to compression by pleural effusions. Worsening compared with CT dated December 03, 2024, with increased extent of changes.

4. No acute focal or infiltrative changes in the lungs.

5. Coronary artery stenting of the right coronary artery, left anterior descending artery, and left circumflex artery.

6. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

7. Left adrenal hyperplasia.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: The membranous portion of the trachea prolapses into the lumen (study performed on incomplete inspiration), respiratory artifacts.
There is interval increase in pleural effusion, on the right measuring up to 2.9 cm  (previously 1.1 cm) and on the left up to 2.1 cm (previously not identified).
The adjacent lung is compressed with subsegmental atelectasis (increased extent compared with CT dated December 02, 2024). Bilateral areas of centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema in upper lobes, scattered cysts in both lungs, air trapping in both upper lobes, unchanged. Reticulonodular changes of the right upper lobe and to a lesser extent in the left upper lobe, with ground-glass and multiple micronodules and interstitial thickening on the background, unchanged. Bronchial walls are diffusely calcified.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass. Lower paratracheal node measures up to 1.1 cm (previously 8 mm).

Heart: The heart is enlarged, with a marked enlargement of both atria, cardiothoracic ratio is 0.55. Pericardial effusion is present, with thickness up to 1.1 cm (previously 6 mm). Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic valve calcifications.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Aortic walls are diffusely calcified. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.2 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Height loss of T6 and T7 vertebral bodies up to 34% (Grade 2 according to Genant classification), unchanged. Decreased density of T11, T12 vertebral bodies up to +83 HU. Moderate degenerative changes of the spine.

Upper abdomen: Left adrenal adenoma measures up to 1.6 cm, unchanged. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: No need for further imaging follow-up. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status.

Thyroid: Heterogeneous and enlarged right lobe of the thyroid gland.
val_impression:
1. Increased bilateral pleural effusion, likely a consequence of congestive cardiac failure, and compressive atelectasis in the lower lobes of both lungs, interval worsening compared to December 4, 2024, September 4, 2024. Reticulonodular changes of the upper lobes with ground-glass and multiple micronodules on the background, stable, may represent post-inflammatory changes. 

2. Moderate mixed emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care. 
Scattered cysts and air trapping in both upper lobes, unchanged.

3. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy due to enlargement of the lower paratracheal node, interval worsening compared to December 4, 2024, likely due to congestive cardiac failure or inflammatory changes.

4. Cardiomegaly. Pericardial effusion, interval worsening compared with CT on December 03, 2024, likely due to congestive cardiac failure. Aortic valve calcifications. Severe aortic calcification.

5. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

6. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.2 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

7. Height loss of T6 and T7 vertebral bodies (Grade 2 according to Genant classification), and decreased density of T11, T12 vertebral bodies may represent osteoporosis.

8. Heterogeneous and enlarged right lobe of the thyroid gland. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound is recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: In the left lower lobe, on image 70, series 5, there is a pleural-based part-solid nodule with spiculated margins, pleural tags extending to the interlobar and costal pleura, and a cystic component, overall measuring 2.4 × 1.8 cm; the solid component measures 8 × 6 mm.
Linear bands in the right lower lobe.
Polymorphous pleural-based opacities with irregular margins in the apical right upper lobe.
Accessory fissure in the right upper lobe.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly.
No significant pericardial effusion.
Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcification.
Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the spine.
Schmorl's nodes of the adjacent endplates of T8 and T9.
Anterior wedge deformity of the T2 vertebral body with vertebral body height loss up to 30%.
Density of vertebral bodies is decreased to 100-130 HU (signs of demineralization).
Moderate bilateral gynecomastia is present.

Upper abdomen: Cystic lesion in the left kidney measuring 1.4 × 1.3 cm, with internal calcification.
doc_impression:
1. Complex nodule in the left lower lobe with features suspicious for neoplasm. Given the patient's high risk (age 75 years, long smoking history, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), this finding, in accordance with the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, requires further evaluation with PET/CT and biopsy.

2. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild.
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Compression fracture of the T2 vertebral body (Genant grade 2).

4. Cystic lesion of the left kidney with internal calcification. Further evaluation with CT with intravenous contrast is recommended. Metallic clips in the gallbladder fossa.

val_impression:

Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT at 3-6 months to confirm persistence. If the nodule is persistent, consider PET/CT, tissue sampling, or surgical resection.

Mitral valve calcifications.

val_protocol:

COMPARISON: Abdomen CT on March 21, 2024.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 11.11 mGy; total DLP 373.18 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:
Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Subsolid nodule measuring 7 mm (previously 9 mm) on image 95.
- Micronodules up to 2 mm, unchanged.

Multiple multiloculated air-filled cysts persist in the lungs, unchanged, located in the right upper and middle lobes and in both lower lobes. Locally dilated lumina of segmental and subsegmental bronchi also persist in the lower lobes, with cystic bronchiectasis.
In the supradiaphragmatic right lower lobe, there is a linear band with a dilated subsegmental bronchial lumen within it.
No pleural effusion.
Mild thickening of the bronchial walls in the lower lobes. Small amount of mural pathologic contents in the tracheal lumen.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:
No mass or lymphadenopathy. Bifurcation lymph nodes are partially calcified.

Heart:
No cardiomegaly. Small pericardial effusion with separation of the pericardial layers up to 5 mm. Coronary calcification is present in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels:
Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.9 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Severe degenerative changes of the spine (osteochondrosis, spondylosis). Osteosclerotic lesion in the L1 vertebral body with well-defined margins, measuring 1.3 × 1.0 cm, density up to 550 HU (unchanged).

Upper abdomen:
Cystic lesion of the right renal sinus measuring 4.5 × 4.0 cm. Decreased density of the liver parenchyma to 48 HU.
Right adrenal lesion with density of -22 HU, measuring 1.5 × 1.4 cm; left adrenal lesion measuring 1.6 × 1.4 cm, density -8 HU. Cystic lesion in the right hepatic lobe measuring 8 × 5 mm, unchanged.
doc_impression:
1. Subsolid nodule in the right upper lobe with partial decrease in size. Decrease in size from 9 mm to 7 mm is a favorable sign; however, given its persistence and size of 6 mm or greater in a high-risk patient, continued follow-up is recommended, with follow-up CT in 12 months per the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations. Stable micronodules 2 mm or smaller do not require dedicated follow-up.

2. Multifocal cystic involvement of the lungs, unchanged.

3. Cystic bronchiectasis in lung bases, unchanged.

4. Mild thickening of the bronchial walls in the lower lobes and mural contents in the tracheal lumen, findings of bronchitis.

5. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild.
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

6. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.9 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

7. Low-density adrenal lesions measuring up to 1.6 cm, likely benign. No additional imaging is required.

8. Cystic lesion in the region of the right renal sinus. Further evaluation is recommended with CT with intravenous contrast.

9. Unchanged cystic lesion of the right hepatic lobe, most likely a simple cyst.

10. Osteosclerotic lesion in the L1 vertebral body. In the absence of oncologic history and given stable size, follow-up with CT or MRI in 12 months may be considered to confirm stability and exclude growth.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Part-solid nodule measures 7 mm (image 95), on Chest CT on May 19, 2025 9 mm, on Chest CT on December 31, 2024 1.1 cm × 0.9 cm.
Right lower lobe:
- Calcified nodule measures 4 mm (image 200), unchanged, healed granuloma, requires no follow-up.
Micronodules up to 2 mm in both lungs, unchanged.
Multiple multiloculated cysts persist in the lungs, unchanged compared to Chest CT on December 31, 2024. Cysts are located peribronchially in the right upper and middle lobes and in both lower lobes, reticulations are noted along the periphery of the cysts. In the upper lobes there is mild centrilobular emphysema, a few areas of paraseptal emphysema (image 84, on the left). A few linear atelectasis in the basal lungs. No pleural effusion. Mild thickening of the bronchial walls in the lower lobes. Small amount of mucus in the tracheal lumen.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass. The mediastinal lymph nodes are numerous, up to 1.0 cm in size; compared to CT on May 19, 2025, an increase in the size of the lymph nodes by 1-2 mm along the short axis is noted. Subcarinal lymph nodes are partially calcified (healed granulomatous disease).

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Pericardial effusion with thickness up to 1.2 cm, unchanged. Coronary calcification is present in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 4.3 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the spine. Osteosclerotic lesion in the L1 vertebral body with well-defined margins, measuring 1.3 × 1.0 cm, 550 HU (unchanged).

Upper abdomen: Cystic lesion of the right renal sinus measuring 4.5 × 4.0 cm. Cystic lesion in the right hepatic lobe measuring 8 × 5 mm, unchanged.
Right adrenal lesion with density of -22 HU, measuring 1.5 × 1.4 cm; diffuse thickening of the left adrenal gland; unchanged.
val_impression:
1. Part-solid nodule in the right upper lobe with decrease in size compared to Chest CT on May 19, 2025, December 31, 2024, probably corresponds to an inflammatory process. Stable 2 mm micronodules do not require follow-up.

2. Diffuse cystic lung disease, unchanged compared to December 31, 2024.

3. Mild centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema in upper lobes. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

4. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery represents pulmonary hypertension, which is consistent with echocardiogram on February 20, 2026.

6. Trace pericardial effusion.

7. Cystic lesion of the right renal sinus. ACR White Paper recommendations (Herts, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2018;15:264-273) suggest the following: Likely benign cyst. No need for further work-up.

8. Unchanged cystic lesion of the right hepatic lobe. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following: For patients with low risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, no need for further follow-up.

9. Right adrenal lesion and diffuse thickening of the left adrenal gland, unchanged. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: Benign lesion. No need for further imaging follow-up.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

Polymorphous linear opacities and perilobular opacities are present in the lungs, greatest in the right middle and lower lobes, as well as linear atelectatic changes in the posterior basal regions. Focally dilated segmental and subsegmental bronchi are noted in areas of linear opacities.
Areas of paraseptal emphysema are present in the upper lungs.
In the supradiaphragmatic right lung, there is an air cyst with irregular contours and thin internal septa, measuring 1.8 × 1.4 cm.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Upper abdomen: In the parenchyma of the right hepatic lobe, there is a low-density lesion with indistinct margins, measuring approximately 2.3 × 2.0 cm.
doc_impression:
1. Solid micronodules in the right lower lobe. CT follow-up in 12 months is recommended in accordance with the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations. Subpleural polygonal nodule in the right lower lobe. No follow-up required.

2. CT appearance of polymorphous linear opacities in the lungs, likely consistent with the subpleural fibrotic type of interstitial lung abnormalities (ILA). Given the accompanying pulmonary nodules and the high risk of progression of both processes, interval follow-up is indicated. Follow-up chest CT in 12 months with baseline pulmonary function assessment is recommended. Clinical and imaging progression, including development of respiratory symptoms, warrants earlier follow-up imaging.

3. Mild paraseptal emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

4. Right hepatic lobe lesion. Per ACR recommendations (2017), further evaluation is required. Liver MRI with intravenous contrast is recommended for differential diagnosis, to exclude a solid neoplasm and further characterize the lesion.

In both lungs, there is irregular increase and distortion of the pulmonary markings, with thickening of the interstitial structures, more pronounced centrally, reticular changes, and areas of consolidation. On this background, lumina of irregularly dilated bronchi are seen; some contain bronchial contents. Compared with CT from December 15, 2025, new bronchial contents are present in one of the largest bronchiectatic airways in the right upper lobe, series 3, image 79; otherwise unchanged. Compared with CT from December 15, 2025, bronchial contents have appeared in a right upper lobe bronchiectatic airway; otherwise unchanged. Compared with CT from August 26, 2025, the areas of consolidation in both lungs have decreased in size, which may have been due to superimposed inflammatory changes on the background of the underlying disease.

Given the presence of consolidation and nodules with subpleural and peribronchial distribution, ground-glass opacities, crazy paving, cryptogenic organizing pneumonia or other interstitial lung diseases are possible. val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Diffuse multiple pulmonary cysts with thin walls and upper lobe predominance. In the right upper lobe, there is a cavity with bronchial connection, irregular wall, solid mural component, and metal foreign body. Overall dimensions are about 2.7 × 2.1 cm (series 5, image 279). A solid component measures 1.9 × 1.0 cm, and a foreign body measures up to 4 mm (3000 HU). There are multiple strands to the costal pleura and to the mediastinal pleura. Adjacent subsegmental bronchi are retracted toward the lesion, and their walls are thickened, with contents in their lumen. Two calcified nodules measure up to 4 mm in both lungs (image 127 on the right and image 78 on the left), healed granulomas, require no follow-up. Mild interstitial changes in lung bases (clinically insignificant). No pleural effusion. Trachea and central bronchi are patent. Wall thickening and secretion of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: Cardiac chambers are enlarged. Aortic valve calcification. Fluid in the pericardial cavity measures up to 1.1 cm in thickness. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated (3.9 cm), with atherosclerotic plaques. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.7 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with ossification of the anterior longitudinal ligament.

Upper abdomen: Multiple hypodense lesions in the liver parenchyma with well-defined smooth contours and fluid density, measuring up to 9 mm. Lymph node in the lesser omentum is enlarged to 1.1 cm in short axis (series 4, image 253), and along the hepatoduodenal ligament to 1.1 cm in short axis (image 249).

Thyroid gland: Asymmetric, left lobe is enlarged, heterogeneous with nodular lesions.
val_impression:
1. The right upper lobe cavity with bronchial connection, irregular wall, solid mural component, and metal foreign body may represent a pulmonary mycetoma (aspergilloma) and requires follow-up.

2. Diffuse cystic lung disease with upper lobe predominance. According to Managing Incidental Findings on Thoracic CT: Lung Findings. A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2021, consider pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis.

3. Wall thickening and secretion of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi may represent bronchitis.

4. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.7 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

5. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Small-volume pericardial effusion.

7. Hypodense liver lesions. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, follow-up MRI in 3 to 6 months is recommended for further evaluation.

8. Abdominal lymphadenopathy.

9. Heterogeneous and enlarged left thyroid lobe. ACR White Paper recommendations (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150) suggest the following: Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound.

Pericardial fluid with thickness up to 5 mm (stable).

In the parenchyma of the right kidney and in its sinus, two hypodense lesions of fluid density with well-defined smooth margins measure 5.6 × 4.0 cm and 4.7 × 3.8 cm, respectively - stable.

## IMPRESSION

1. Compared with January 09, 2026, complete regression of areas of pulmonary parenchymal opacity of consolidation type, as well as solid and subsolid nodules (in the left lower lobe and right middle lobe) - changes were likely inflammatory.  
A partially calcified solid nodule in the right upper lobe (typical of a healed granuloma), and multiple pulmonary calcifications (stable): per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, no further CT follow-up is required.  
Pulmonary emphysema with formation of a single bulla on the right (per prior examinations, the patient is enrolled in a lung cancer screening program).

3. Aortic valve calcification.

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophytes.  
Concrescence of vertebral bodies T1-T2.  
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-L1 level.

6. Hypodense fluid-density lesions in the right kidney: per “Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee” (2018), CT follow-up is not required.

No new nodules are identified; multiple scattered calcified nodules and solid micronodules, unchanged. Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingula, and basal segments bilaterally. No pleural effusion. The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass is identified. Right lower paratracheal node measures 1.0 cm along the short axis, previously 1.2 cm (on January 9, 2026). Subcarinal node measures 5 mm along the short axis, previously 9 mm (on January 9, 2026).

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified; the right atrium is decreased in size. No significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes. Block of T1-T2 vertebrae. Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-L1 level up to +117 HU (CT signs of demineralization).

Upper abdomen: In the parenchyma of the right kidney and in its sinus, two hypodense lesions of fluid density with well-defined smooth margins measure 5.6 × 4.0 cm and 4.7 × 3.8 cm, stable compared to January 9, 2026 (at CT on November 19, 2024, outside the scanning zone).  Cholecystectomy.
val_impression:
1. Compared with January 9, 2026, complete regression of bilateral areas of ground-glass and consolidation, as well as solid and subsolid nodules in the right middle lobe (inflammatory process). In the right upper lobe, subpleural solid nodule measures up to 4 mm, unchanged. Multiple scattered calcified nodules and solid micronodules, unchanged. No new nodules are identified. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up for patients with high or low risk of lung cancer.

2. Moderate pulmonary emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

3. Hypodense fluid-density lesions in the right kidney, unchanged. ACR White Paper recommendations (Herts, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2018;15:264-273) suggest the following: Likely benign cyst. No need for further work-up.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Initial follow-up chest CT at 3-6 months to confirm persistence. If stable, follow-up chest CT every 2 years until 5 years. 
Two solid nodules of the right lung up to 5 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Mild paraseptal emphysema, moderate centrilobular emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

3. Scattered pulmonary cysts may be clinically insignificant or consistent with the signs of lymphangioleiomyomatosis.

4. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

6. Left adrenal lesion with benign features (adenoma). ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: Benign adenoma. No need for further imaging follow-up. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

doc_findings:
Severe respiratory motion artifacts. Visualization is markedly limited.

Lungs and pleura: Considering the artifacts described above, reliable assessment of nodules per the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations is not possible. No large nodules are identified. A few calcifications in the left lung measure up to 4 mm.

The contour of the right hemidiaphragm is deformed: In the anterior portions, a defect is noted measuring approximately 5.5 cm on the sagittal image, with herniation of abdominal organs (small bowel loops with mesentery, ascending and transverse colon, and omental fat) into the right hemithorax by nearly 1/2 of its volume, without evidence of ascitic fluid within the herniation sac.  
The right lung is compressed by the hernia predominantly in the lower segments in lung bases and in the right middle lobe. The right upper lobe shows hypoventilatory changes.

No pleural effusion.  
The trachea and main bronchi (except for the atelectatic areas described above) are patent.  
Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: The mediastinum is shifted to the left. No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification is identified.  
Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.  
Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. Decreased density of bone tissue in the vertebrae at the T11 level up to +131 HU.  
Healing fracture of the posterior segment of the right 10th rib.

Upper abdomen (except as described above): Unremarkable.

INDICATION: LUNG NODULE. ABNORMAL XR.

COMPARISON: Chest Xray 10/15/2025.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 14.45 mGy; total DLP 517.25 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:
Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and in lung bases bilaterally.
There are persistent nodules of varying density in the lungs, and a focal area of consolidation in the right upper lobe:

In the right lung:
- In the upper lobe:
  - Focal consolidation with linear margins, adjacent to the apical pleura, measuring about 3.7 × 2.7 cm (no change), with traction and deformation of the adjacent small bronchi (the lumens of the latter are not visualized within the lesion).
  - Subpleural solid nodule measuring up to 4 mm, partially calcified, polygonal in shape (series 5, image 101), with no change.
  Subsolid nodule measuring up to 4 mm (series 5, image 70), with no change.
- In the middle lobe:
  - Perifissural solid nodule measuring up to 8 mm, with no change.

In the left lung:
- In the upper lobe:
  - Subpleural (series 5, image 47) ground-glass nodule of moderate intensity, measuring about 1.7 × 1.4 cm, with no change.
  - Subsolid nodule adjacent to the interlobar pleura (the latter is retracted), measuring about 1.4 × 1.1 cm; margins are linear, with no change.
- In the lower lobe:
  - Subpleural solid nodule measuring up to 6 mm (series 5, image 120), with no change.

No pleural effusion.
The trachea and central bronchi (except as described above) are patent; walls are thickened.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass is identified. Station 7 lymph node measures up to 1.2 cm in short axis, with no change.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Aortic valve calcification. Moderate coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.0 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased bone tissue density in vertebrae at the T11-L2 level down to +113 HU (CT signs of osteopenia). Osteoscleroic lesions in the posterior segment of the left 8th rib measuring up to 9 mm, with no change.

Upper abdomen:
In the renal parenchyma, hypodense lesions with sharp smooth margins and fluid density measure up to 5.9 × 5.7 cm on the right and 3.8 × 3.1 cm on the left (no change).
Increase in size of a lymph node along the hepatoduodenal ligament to 1.2 cm in short axis, previously up to 8 mm.
Wall calcification of the abdominal aorta and its branches at the imaged level.
Increase in the splenic index to 650, with mild interval increase.
Portal vein dilation to 1.7 cm (no change), and splenic vein dilation to 1.0 cm (previously up to 9 mm).
doc_impression:
1. CT signs of focal apical right lung consolidation, with no change since 2024; correlation with clinical history is required to differentiate primarily between scarring, post-therapeutic changes (as a consequence of antitumor therapy) versus a lung mass with stable size. Fleischner Society recommendations from 2017 may not be applicable considering the clinical history. CT signs of pulmonary nodules of varying density, with no change. CT signs of bronchial wall thickening (may be a sign of bronchitis).

2. Moderate coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.0 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Thoracic aortic atherosclerosis.

6. Single enlarged intrathoracic lymph node (station 7), with no change.

7. Enlarged lymph node along the hepatoduodenal ligament (previously smaller), increased spleen size, dilation of the portal (stable) and splenic (with mild increase) veins; to clarify the nature of the changes, an abdominal organ study and correlation with follow-up studies are recommended. Hypodense renal lesions of fluid density are stable (likely cysts): per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2018, CT follow-up is not required.

8. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization) at T11-L2. Osteoscleroic lesions in the left 8th rib, with no change (likely degenerative).

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: In the apex of the right lung, a spiculated solid mass measures 3.4 × 2.3 × 1.7 cm, with strands to the pleura, with a single 1 mm calcification, deformation and interruption of the small bronchi. On December 11, 2024, the mass measured 3.1 × 2.4 × 1.8 cm, had a larger number of spicules and a ground-glass area on the periphery, and was without calcification.
In the left upper lobe, a subpleural area of ground-glass is preserved (series 5, image 47), measuring about 1.7 × 1.4 cm, with no change. 
An area of consolidation and ground glass in the left upper lobe adjacent to the interlobar pleura (series 5, images 66-83), with small bronchiectasis, measures 2.0 × 1.1 cm (previously 1.5 × 1.0 cm). The consolidation part has increased in comparison with chest CT on December 11, 2024. 
In the lower parts, there is thickening of the interlobular interstitium, reticular changes, and mosaic pneumatization. On the right, there are supradiaphragmatic centrilobular ground-glass nodules (tree-in-bud) (series 5, images 198-217), which are not previously identified. 
In the lung parenchyma, multiple nodules are identified, in series 5 presented as follows:
 In the right upper lobe:
- Calcified nodule measures 3 mm (series 5, image 30), with no change.
- Subpleural ground-glass nodule measures 3 mm (series 5, image 71), with no change.
In the right middle lobe:
- Perifissural solid nodule measures up to 8 mm (series 5, image 157), with no change.
In the right lower lobe:
 - A few subpleural solid nodules measure up to 3 mm (series 5, images 157-161), with no change.
In the left upper lobe:
- Subpleural solid nodule measures up to 3 mm (series 5, image 163), with no change.
In the left lower lobe:
- Subpleural solid nodule measures up to 6 mm (series 5, image 120), with no change.
Multiple calcified and solid micronodules up to 2 mm in both lungs with no change. 
No pleural effusion. Wall thickening of the subsegmental and segmental bronchi with secretion. The wall of the trachea and large bronchi is severely thickened.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass is identified. Subcarinal lymph node (station 7 lymph node) measures up to 1.2 cm in short axis, with no change. Other lymph nodes are multiple, up to 9 mm.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.1 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased bone tissue density in vertebrae at the T11-L2 level down to +113 HU, numerous rounded areas of low density in the vertebral bodies, previously identified. Osteoscleroic lesions on the right at the 7th rib, on the left at the 8th rib, with no change.

Upper abdomen: In the renal parenchyma, hypodense lesions with smooth margins and fluid density measure up to 5.9 × 5.7 cm on the right and 3.8 × 3.1 cm on the left (no change). Increase in size of a lymph node along the hepatoduodenal ligament to 1.2 cm in short axis, previously up to 8 mm. Increased spleen size, compared to December 11, 2024. Wall calcification of the abdominal aorta and its branches. Portal vein dilation to 1.7 cm (no change), and splenic vein dilation to 1.0 cm (previously up to 9 mm).
val_impression:
1. In the apex of the right lung, neoplasm without signs of growth, with slight positive dynamics compared to CT on December 11, 2024. Subcarinal lymph node enlargement, with no change.

2. Interval worsening in the form of an increase in the size of the consolidation area in the left upper lobe and the appearance of bronchiolitis in the supradiaphragmatic regions on the right. These changes may represent an inflammatory process. 
Multiple nodules with no change. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history. Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.

3. Bronchial secretion and wall thickening may represent bronchitis. Wall thickening of the trachea and large bronchi may correspond to the large airway disease.

4. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.1 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. Enlarged lymph node along the hepatoduodenal ligament (previously smaller), increased spleen size, dilation of the portal (stable) and splenic (with mild increase) veins. Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.
Hypodense renal lesions of fluid density are stable (likely cysts). Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018 may not be applicable considering the clinical history.


DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Hypoventilatory changes in the lower basal portions of the lungs.

In the subpleural portions of the lungs, areas of paraseptal emphysema persist with areas of fibrosis (more pronounced in the upper lobes of the lungs, honeycombing pattern), visually without interval change, with single air bullae (largest along the right interlobar fissure up to 1.5 × 1.4 cm, without interval change).

In the lung parenchyma, single solid nodules are present, in series 5 as follows:
In the right upper lobe:
* Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 93), without interval change.
In the right lower lobe:
* Nodule measuring up to 7 mm (image 121), without interval change.
In the left lower lobe:
* Nodule measuring up to 1.1 × 0.9 cm with lobulated margins (image 74), adjacent to the interlobar fissure, not previously identified.

No pleural effusion. In the posterior basal portions, an area of costal pleural thickening persists with numerous large calcifications within it; an area of calcification of the right interlobar fissure, visually without interval change.

The trachea and main bronchi are patent, with focal wall calcifications. The transverse tracheal lumen is mildly narrowed and the anteroposterior dimension is widened (saber-sheath trachea), visually without interval change.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Multiple healing rib fractures (more on the left). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes. Decreased height and wedge deformity of the T7 vertebral body up to 33% without spinal canal narrowing at this level. Decreased height of the C5-C7 vertebral bodies up to 45%. Mild decreased density (+145 HU) of the T12 vertebral bone tissue, findings of osteopenia.

Included upper abdomen: Small calcifications partially included within the scan field at the level of the upper and middle calyx groups of the right kidney up to 3 mm.
doc_impression:

Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, short-interval CT follow-up (in 3 months) is recommended, and consider positron emission tomography CT and/or tissue sampling. Other single solid lung nodules show no interval change.

2. CT findings of pulmonary emphysema with severe fibrotic changes, and saber-sheath trachea, findings of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Right pleural thickening with areas of calcification, which may correspond to postinflammatory changes, visually without interval change.

3. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Wedge deformity of the T7 and C5-C7 vertebral bodies (without findings of significant spinal canal narrowing at this level) which may be a sign of osteoporosis, and CT findings of osteopenia at T12. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes. Multiple healing rib fractures bilaterally.

7. Calcifications partially included in the region of the right renal calyces (calculi versus vascular calcifications or calcifications of sinus elements). Given partial visualization, reliable assessment is limited.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Multiple peribronchial subsolid nodules are present in both lungs, 2–5 mm, forming a tree-in-bud pattern (more pronounced in the right lower lobe and in the left upper lobe).

Also in the parenchyma of the left lung, solitary solid nodules are present:
* A nodule in the upper lobe measuring 1.0 × 8 mm on image 81 (series 6).
* A nodule in the upper lobe measuring 5 mm along the minor interlobar fissure on image 106 (series 6).

The trachea and central bronchi are patent. The bronchial walls are thickened. Deformity and dilatation of most subsegmental bronchi and their branches are present, with cylindrical bronchiectasis, more pronounced in the right middle lobe (with decreased right middle lobe volume) and in the left lower lobe.

Areas of linear and focal pulmonary fibrosis are present with formation of traction bronchiectasis perifissural bilaterally.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass is identified. No lymphadenopathy is identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Wall calcification is present. The pulmonary trunk is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with sclerosis of the subchondral portions of the vertebral endplates. Decreased attenuation of vertebrae T12 and L2 to 140 Hounsfield units, and L1 to 133 Hounsfield units (changes are consistent with osteopenia).

Included portions of the abdomen: Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. Multiple peribronchial subsolid pulmonary nodules with a tree-in-bud pattern. Multiple pulmonary bronchiectasis with decreased volume of the right middle lobe (middle lobe syndrome). Per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, repeat computed tomography in 3–6 months is recommended. The above changes require correlation with history and clinical and laboratory data, to differentiate residual postinflammatory changes versus chronic inflammatory changes, including nontuberculous mycobacterial infection. Solitary solid nodules in the left lung. Per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, repeat computed tomography in 3 months is recommended.

2. Mild coronary artery calcification. Consultation with the treating physician is recommended for comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or for semiquantitative scoring of coronary calcification for each of the four major coronary arteries.

3. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Computed tomography findings of osteopenia at T12–L2.

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura: Calcified micronodules in the left lung. Perifissural calcified nodule up to 7 mm in the right upper lobe. No nodules requiring follow-up.
Thin linear atelectasis in segment 5 of the left upper lobe. On expiratory imaging, bilateral lobular air trapping is present. Gravity-dependent changes in the basal regions of the lungs. Thickening of the walls of segmental and subsegmental bronchi. 
No pleural effusion. 

Mediastinum and hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified. Single microcalcification in a paratracheal lymph node. Small hiatal hernia.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Pacemaker. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Severe coronary artery calcification. Moderate aortic valve calcification and severe mitral valve calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated (34 mm).

Osseous structures and chest wall: In the L2 vertebral body, there is a rounded osteosclerotic lesion without well-defined margins, measuring up to 2.3 × 1.3 × 1.8 cm, without cortical disruption or alteration of the vertebral anatomy (unchanged in size compared with the prior study). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

Upper abdomen: Cholecystectomy.
val_impression:
1. Findings of terminal bronchiolar obstruction with bilateral lobular air trapping, which may be consistent with bronchial asthma. Thickening of the walls of segmental and subsegmental bronchi. 

2. Bilateral calcified pulmonary granulomas. No nodules requiring follow-up are identified, resolution of the previously seen pulmonary nodules compared with April 05, 2024.

3. There are no signs of interstitial lung disease.

4. Severe coronary artery calcification. Moderate aortic valve calcification and severe mitral valve calcification. Moderate atherosclerotic calcification of the thoracic aorta.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (34 mm) may be consistent with pulmonary hypertension.

6. Osteoblastic lesion in the L2 vertebral body, unchanged compared with the prior study.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

COMPARISON:  Chest CT on {comparison_date}

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 23.34 mGy; total DLP 826.98 mGy-cm

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: The study is performed with hypoventilatory changes in the posterior basal lung regions. Subsegmental atelectasis in the right middle lobe.  
No nodules requiring follow-up are identified. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No lymphadenopathy is identified. The fat of the upper mediastinum is diffusely increased in attenuation (due to residual thymic tissue?), otherwise no visible masses.

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification is identified.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with subchondral sclerosis of the vertebral endplates and osteophytes.  
In the T10 vertebral body, a sclerotic focus with irregular margins measures about 2.1 × 1.8 × 0.9 cm, attenuation about +1350 Hounsfield units, homogeneous structure.

Included upper abdomen: A hypodense fluid-attenuation lesion is subcapsular in liver segment 2, up to 1.0 × 0.7 cm, with sharp smooth margins. Along the liver capsule at the level of segment 4, single punctate calcifications.

Thyroid gland: Heterogeneous due to a hypodense nodule in the left lobe with attenuation about +40 Hounsfield units, measuring about 1.2 × 1.0 cm, with calcifications on both sides.  
Breasts: Partially included in the scan field, in the posterior third of the right breast at the junction of the upper quadrants, a mass is identified measuring about 1.5 × 1.5 cm, with sharp smooth margins.
doc_impression:
1. No pulmonary nodules requiring further follow-up per the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations are identified.

2. Right breast mass. Correlate with dedicated breast imaging.

3. Sclerotic focus in the T10 vertebral body. Correlate with clinical history (in the absence of clinical manifestations and oncologic status: Bone Reporting and Data System category 1). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

4. Diffuse increased attenuation of the upper mediastinal fat may correspond to residual thymic tissue. Consider follow-up computed tomography in 6 to 12 months.

5. Thyroid nodules. Per “Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee 2014”, thyroid ultrasound is recommended.

6. Hypodense lesion in the left liver lobe. Per “Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on Computed Tomography: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee 2017”, follow-up computed tomography is not required.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Few solid nodules in both lungs: 
- in the right middle lobe 4 mm (image 113, series 4);
- in the right lower lobe 2 mm (image 131, series 4);
- in the right lower lobe 3 mm (image 147, series 4);
- in the left upper lobe 2 mm (image 59, series 4).
Subsegmental atelectasis in the right middle lobe. No consolidation. On the right, the interlobar pleura is minimally thickened, possibly due to tiny amount of fluid.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No lymphadenopathy is identified. Residual thymic tissue in the prevascular space, no masses.

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification is identified.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: In the T10 vertebral body and left pedicle, an osteoblastic lesion with irregular margins measures 2.1 × 1.8 × 0.9 cm, about +1350 HU. In the T7 vertebral body there are two osteoblastic lesions up to 2 mm.
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with subchondral sclerosis of the vertebral endplates and osteophytes.  

Upper abdomen: A hypodense fluid-attenuation lesion is subcapsular in liver segment 2, up to 1.0 × 0.7 cm, with sharp smooth margins. Along the liver capsule at the level of segment 4, single punctate calcifications.

Thyroid gland: Hypodense nodule in the left lobe measuring about 1.2 × 1.0 cm, hypodense lesion in the lower pole of the left lobe up to 4 mm.

Breasts: in the posterior third of the right breast, anterior to the pectoral muscle, a solid mass is identified, measuring 2.0 × 1.5 × 1.5 cm, with sharp irregular margins.
val_impression:
1. Few solid nodules in both lungs up to 4 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Right breast mass in the posterior third of the right breast, anterior to the pectoral muscle. Correlate with dedicated breast imaging.

3. Osteoblastic lesions of T10 and T7. Correlate with clinical history (oncologic status).

4. Mild aortic calcification. No coronary calcification is identified.

5. Thyroid nodules of left lobe, less than 1.5 cm. ACR White Paper recommendations (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.

6. Hypodense lesion in the left liver lobe. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following:
For patients with low risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, no need for further follow-up. For patients with high risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, follow-up MRI in 3 to 6 months is recommended for further evaluation.

doc_findings:
FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura: Bilateral multisegmental, predominantly subpleural, reticular changes of the lung parenchyma with mosaic attenuation, with associated traction bronchiectasis of medium-order bronchi. Total volume of involved lung parenchyma is 17% (previously 12%). No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and hila: No mass. Enlarged subaortic lymph node up to 1.1 cm in short axis (previously 1.0 cm), series 3 image 71. New enlarged prevertebral lymph node up to 1.1 cm in short axis (series 3 image 69). Small hiatal hernia persists, measuring up to 4.0 cm in the axial plane.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Moderate calcification of the aortic and mitral valves. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Healed fracture of the anterior third of the left sixth rib. Decreased trabecular attenuation of the vertebral bodies to 92 Hounsfield units (previously 98 Hounsfield units). Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

Included upper abdomen: Round radiopaque calculus within the gallbladder lumen measuring up to 1.9 cm. Round calcification in the splenic parenchyma measuring up to 7 mm.
doc_impression:
1. Pulmonary fibrotic changes, computed tomography stage 1, with mild increase in the volume of involved lung parenchyma (17% versus 12%), compared with November 03, 2025.

2. Findings of terminal airway obstruction, which may correspond to chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

3. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy with interval size increase, compared with November 03, 2025.

4. Small hiatal hernia, unchanged.

5. Moderate calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.

6. Healed fracture of the anterior third of the left sixth rib. Findings of osteoporosis. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

7. Cholelithiasis. Splenic parenchymal calcification.

8. Overall negative radiographic change, compared with November 03, 2025.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:  
In segment 5 of the right lung, linear areas of parenchymal consolidation, likely fibrotic changes. In the right lung, solitary perifissural polygonal nodules up to 3 mm, likely intrapulmonary lymph nodes.  
In the left lung, an accessory interlobar fissure.  
No nodules requiring follow-up are identified.  
No pleural effusion.  
Walls of the subsegmental bronchi are mildly thickened, and isolated bronchi contain material.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary arteries with isolated calcified atherosclerotic plaques.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcification of the aortic arch and descending thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.2 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall:  
Vertebral bodies are mildly flattened. Subchondral sclerosis of adjacent endplates is present. Marginal anterior and lateral osteophytes along the vertebral body margins are present. Disruption of the endplates of isolated vertebral bodies consistent with Schmorl nodes. Intervertebral disc height is decreased, with vacuum phenomenon at T11–T12. Hemangioma in the T11 vertebral body.  
Attenuation of the T12 vertebra is decreased to 92 Hounsfield units. Fractures of the posterior segments of the left 10th and 11th ribs with signs of consolidation.

Included portions of the abdomen: In the parenchyma of the right hepatic lobe, a subtotally calcified lesion up to 1.0 cm. In the body of the pancreas, a hypodense area with smooth sharp margins up to 1.0 cm in diameter, attenuation -60 Hounsfield units. Cholecystectomy.
doc_impression:
1. No findings to suggest pneumonia.

2. Mild thickening of the walls of the subsegmental bronchi and material in their lumens, consistent with bronchitis.

3. Localized pulmonary fibrosis in the right middle lobe.

4. Mild coronary artery calcification. Recommend consultation with the treating physician for comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or semiquantitative coronary artery calcium scoring for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Aortic calcification.

6. Main pulmonary artery dilation. Recommend clinical correlation for pulmonary arterial hypertension.

7. Fat-attenuation focus in the pancreatic body, likely focal lipomatosis.

8. Solitary hepatic calcification.

9. Hemangioma in the T11 vertebral body.

10. Degenerative changes of the spine. Findings of osteoporosis.

11. Consolidated fractures of the left 10th and 11th ribs.

doc_findings:
FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura: Mild centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema in the apices. A single thin-walled subpleural bulla in the right upper lobe, segment 1, measuring 2.2 × 1.5 cm. In the apical portion of the left upper lobe, segments 1 and 2, a calcified granuloma measuring 4 × 3 mm. Aeration of the lung parenchyma is heterogeneous. Respiratory motion artifacts are present in the lung bases. Linear atelectasis in the left upper lobe, segment 5. Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe and in the lingular and basal segments of the left lung. No pleural effusion.

Trachea: The membranous portion of the trachea prolapses into the lumen (study performed on incomplete inspiration). Small amount of adherent intraluminal content within the trachea.

Mediastinum and hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Mild coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Atherosclerotic calcification of the wall. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Decreased height and wedge deformity of the T11 vertebral body up to 25 percent (Genant grade 1). Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Diffuse calcification of the anterior longitudinal ligament. Ankylosis of the right seventh costovertebral joint.

Upper abdomen (included portions): Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up according to Fleischner Society recommendations.

2. Mild mixed emphysema. Single thin-walled subpleural bulla in the right upper lobe, segment 1, measuring 2.2 × 1.5 cm.

3. Calcified granuloma in the left upper lobe (segments 1 and 2), consistent with prior granulomatous disease.

4. Mild coronary artery calcification.

5. T11 vertebral body compression fracture with up to 25 percent height loss (Genant grade 1). Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with diffuse calcification of the anterior longitudinal ligament.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Right lung is decreased in volume, with areas of linear and bandlike fibrosis; history of surgical treatment for lung cancer, and the lower lobe is compensatorily hyperexpanded. Along the suture material at the level of the stump of the right upper lobar bronchus, there is an area of consolidation of nonmeasurable size, irregular in shape with bandlike margins, involving the perihilar portions of the right lung. No significant bronchial narrowing is noted on this background, and the lumens are patent.  
In the left lung parenchyma, there are a few nodules, on series 5 presented as follows:  
In the left upper lobe:  
* Solid nodule up to 4 mm (image 134).  
* Solid nodule up to 3 mm (image 100).  
In the left lower lobe:  
* Subsolid nodule up to 7 mm (image 146).  
In the lung parenchyma, there are areas of paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema. In the lower basal portions of the left lung, there are areas of mild ground-glass opacity with sharp margins.  
Pleural effusion: On the right, in the residual pleural space, there is a small amount of fluid (thickness about 1.2 cm); on the left, there is no effusion. Right pleura is thickened up to 8 mm; left pleura shows no visible change.

Mediastinum and lung hila: Mediastinum is mildly shifted to the right. Mediastinal lymph nodes are numerous, the largest short axis up to 1.4 cm (4R group). Right bronchopulmonary lymph nodes are difficult to differentiate on the background of the above-described area of consolidation.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is dilated in the ascending portion up to 4.0 cm, with wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.7 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Decreased vertebral body height at T6–T9 up to 32%, decreased vertebral density at T11–L1 (58–61 Hounsfield units). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.  
Areas of sclerotic bone remodeling in the anterior portions of the right fifth and sixth ribs, and in the posterior portion of the right eighth rib. Fractures of the posterior portions of the right sixth and seventh ribs with displacement of bone fragments and pseudoarthrosis formation.

Included abdomen: In the mid third of the right kidney parenchyma in the posterior segment, a hypodense lesion with sharp smooth margins and fluid attenuation, measuring about 2.0 × 1.5 cm.

Included neck: Right supraclavicular lymph nodes up to 1.0 cm in short axis.
doc_impression:
1. Status post surgical treatment for right lung cancer of unknown date. Computed tomography findings of an area of consolidation in the perihilar portions of the right lung; findings require correlation with clinical history and prior computed tomography, suspicious for recurrence; postoperative changes are less likely. Recommendation: Thoracic surgery consultation and bronchoscopy.  

2. Few solid and one subsolid lesions in the left lung. Fleischner Society recommendations cannot be applied given the clinical history. Follow-up should be performed per the oncologic surveillance protocol. Areas of emphysema. Small effusion in the residual right pleural space, and right costal pleural thickening (postoperative etiology?). Areas of mild ground-glass opacity in the left lower lobe (possibly postinflammatory).  

3. Mediastinal and right supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.  

4. Dilatation of the main pulmonary artery, which may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.  

5. Moderate coronary artery calcification.  

6. Aortic valve calcification.  

7. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta.  

8. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased height of the T6–T9 vertebral bodies, computed tomography findings of osteoporosis. Areas of rib sclerosis of indeterminate nature (consider the question of performing bone scintigraphy), and right sixth and seventh rib fractures with pseudoarthrosis formation.  

9. Hypodense fluid-attenuation lesion of the right kidney: Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on Computed Tomography: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee 2018 is not applicable (given the history of oncologic disease).

Stump of the right upper lobar bronchus with thickening of the wall. Along the suture material in the right middle lobe there is an area of consolidation with smooth edges, 5.0*2.3 cm. 
Moderate paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema. In the lower lobes of both lungs there are multiple centrilobular areas of ground glass, mosaic pneumatization with air traps, the contents in the lumens of the subsegmental bronchi (small airways disease). In the lower lobe of the right lung there are linear opacities, may be an inflammatory process or hypoventilatory atelectasis. In the left lung subpleural reticulation and ground glass, consistent with interstitial changes.
In the left lung, there are a few nodules, on series 5 presented as follows:  
In the left upper lobe:  
* Solid nodule up to 4 mm (image 134).  
* Solid nodule up to 3 mm (image 100).  
In the left lower lobe:  
* Subsolid nodule up to 7 mm (image 146).  
On the right, there is a small amount of fluid in the pleural space (thickness about 1.2 cm); on the left, there is no effusion. Right pleura is thickened up to 8 mm.

Mediastinum and lung hila: Mediastinum is mildly shifted to the right. Mediastinal lymph nodes are numerous, the largest short axis up to 1.4 cm (4R group). Right bronchopulmonary lymph nodes are difficult to differentiate on the background of probable invasion of the mass.

Heart: No cardiomegaly, left ventricular enlargement. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased height of the T6–T9 vertebral bodies, computed tomography findings of osteoporosis. 

8. Hypodense fluid-attenuation lesion of the right kidney: Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on Computed Tomography: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee 2018 is not applicable (given the history of oncologic disease).

2. Mild, upper lobe predominant paraseptal emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

Atherosclerotic calcification of the thoracic and abdominal aorta and branches, with dilation of the ascending aorta (4.2 cm). Enlarged right pulmonary artery.

val_impression:
1. Multiloculated cyst in the right upper lobe measures 1.2 × 1.2 × 1.0 cm. According to ACR Lung-RADS v2022 multilocular cysts are classified as Lung-RADS 4A with recommended management of 3-month CT or PET/CT if there is a solid component of at least 8 mm.

2. Multiple solid and subsolid nodules in the upper lobes. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT at 3-6 months to confirm persistence. If stable, consider chest CT at 2 and 4 years.

3. Heterogeneous pleural thickening on the right measures 3.0 × 5.4 × 1.2 cm, contains fat and solid components, may correspond to neoplasm. Compared to the Abdomen CT on 2025-09-12, there is marked interval worsening due to the development of a solid component.

4. Mild paraseptal emphysema in the apices. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

5. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

7. Left adrenal lesion measuring < 1 cm. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following:  No need for further imaging follow-up.

8. Hepatic cysts. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following: Benign lesion. No need for further follow-up.

2. Compared to the prior study, there are new right upper lobe nodules forming a "tree-in-bud" pattern, suggestive of bronchiolitis. The remaining pulmonary nodules are stable or demonstrate a minimal increase in size. Pulmonary emphysema. Bilateral atelectasis.

Moderate aortic valve calcification. Moderate atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta, abdominal aorta, and branches.
